title: Court Reporting and Stenotype Services

narrative.executive_view: This is a conditional, heterogeneous roll-up candidate rather than a clean AI-cost-takeout. NAICS 561492 covers verbatim reporting, stenotype recording of live legal proceedings and subsequent transcription; it also includes realtime captioning [S1]. AI and digital reporting can compress transcription, scheduling and QA cost, but a paid human record-capture, certification and liability function remains in important settings. The attractive targets are agencies with transferable law-firm relationships, a durable reporter roster and a credible digital workflow; a thin freelancer network or one-off client book can lose the very assets a buyer believes it is acquiring.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI speech-to-text can create a fast first draft, and digital reporting already separates capture from transcription. AAERT's certifications explicitly cover trained electronic and deposition reporters who digitally capture proceedings and monitor audio in real time [S2]. That is meaningful substitution for stenographic production and post-production labor, not proof that the human role disappears: audio quality, speaker management, oath/certification rules, chain of custody, realtime service and legal accountability still require a qualified person. The supplied 4-digit 5614 role mix is badly unrepresentative because it omits court reporters, so V is necessarily a conservative proxy judgment.
narrative.value_capture: Agencies may retain part of the savings through scheduling, workflow, bundled videography and client-service infrastructure, but page-rate and competitive procurement limit the spread. Federal-court examples show per-page pricing and separate rates by turnaround and copy type [S3], illustrating that a production-cost reduction need not automatically raise agency revenue. The durable agency advantage is preferred-vendor access, reliable capture and delivery, not ownership of the underlying speech-recognition technology. I therefore assign moderate distribution and moat factors rather than treating certification as a complete pricing shield.
narrative.adoption_timing: Effective adoption reaches 50% of eligible workflow in about four years from a current 15% base. That reflects widespread digital-capture capability and AI drafting, offset by state-level variation, high-stakes accuracy requirements, reporter acceptance and the difference between a useful draft and a legally defensible record. The source evidence establishes a real digital reporting pathway [S2], but it does not measure AI penetration among 561492 agencies; both current adoption and timing are estimates and must not be read as an industry survey result.
narrative.consolidation_economics: Buyability is genuine but uneven. The court-reporting advisory source lists 2025 acquisitions by Veritext, Magna and Lexitas and describes a long-running consolidation dynamic [S5]. An independent 2026 valuation guide places $500K-$2M EBITDA regional court-reporting firms at 4.5x-6.5x adjusted EBITDA and larger multi-market platforms at 7x-10x [S4]. The dataset's $1-10M band straddles those categories, so I use 5.0x entry and 5.5x exit only for a quality regional operator that adds a transferable roster and recurring law-firm relationships. Those are source-informed estimates, not disclosed terms for a particular NAICS transaction.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not melting. The code's paid output is a verbatim legal record and related live reporting, not merely generic transcription [S1]. AAERT describes digital reporters as a scalable response to reporter shortages while retaining real-time capture, protocols and accuracy responsibilities [S2]. The human production layer can narrow to capture, verification and certification as AI handles more text, and lower-value captioning/transcription may commoditize. However, the residual legal-record and high-stakes realtime service should preserve paid demand in a substantial subset of the market.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The dominant risks are scope and asset-transfer risk. The deterministic wage mix is a 4-digit contact-center proxy with no court-reporter SOC, while much agency production may be paid to 1099 reporters outside W-2 wages; the labor-share input therefore understates and misclassifies economics. NAICS also includes realtime captioning, whose demand and pricing may differ from deposition reporting. The valuation range is a commercial guide rather than disclosed target transactions, and each target requires diligence on reporter agreements, client concentration, state rules, WIP/receivables, digital workflow and the share of revenue that remains after AI-assisted transcription.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 561492 Court Reporting and Stenotype Services
sources.what_it_supports: Exact NAICS scope: verbatim reporting and stenotype recording of live legal proceedings plus transcription of recorded materials; the code includes realtime captioning examples.

sources.title: AAERT: About AAERT Certifications
sources.what_it_supports: Digital reporting and transcription certification pathway, real-time audio monitoring, and the association's description of stenographer shortages and digital reporting as a scalable alternative.

sources.title: Northern District of California: Transcript Pricing Explanation & Examples
sources.what_it_supports: Primary federal-court examples of per-page transcript pricing and delivery/copy-rate distinctions; court pricing is not a complete proxy for private deposition pricing.

sources.title: Court Reporting Firm Valuation: 4.5-10.5x EBITDA (2026)
sources.what_it_supports: Commercial valuation guide's stated 2026 ranges for regional and platform court-reporting firms, used as a disclosed third-party range rather than as a transaction database.

sources.title: Jackim Woods: Court Reporting Acquisitions in 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Commercial M&A advisory source describing court-reporting consolidation and listing 2025 acquisitions by national and regional buyers.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561492 ($800,378,213) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561492 ($3,128,565,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Mail Clerks and Mail Machine Operators, Except Postal Service

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $242,882 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561492); class <5: 2,701 firms, avg revenue/firm $234,096 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,636 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 164 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,455,720 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $227,820 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 72 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,802,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $438,513 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 51 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,039,745 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,101,720 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $46,248,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,237,899 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $182,932,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $28,628,925 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 60
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AI/digital workflows materially expose transcription, coordination, QA and routine administration, while certified capture, legal accountability and realtime delivery retain a human floor. Because the supplied 4-digit role proxy omits court reporters, the aggregate intentionally remains conservative and low-confidence.
caveats: The supplied 4-digit 5614 occupations are a poor proxy for 561492 and omit court reporters/stenographers.
Freelance and 1099 reporter payouts may be a larger production cost than the W-2 wage-based labor-share input.
The role arithmetic is required against the supplied proxy and is not observed 561492 occupational data.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, client communication and production coordination partly automate; exception handling persists.
This is proxy contamination; routine scripted outreach would be exposed if present.
This is proxy contamination; documentation can automate but regulated exception work remains.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, QA workflows and reporting automate partly; production responsibility remains.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research and proposal drafting automate, but law-firm relationship selling remains.
Reporting and forecasting automate while legal-service accountability and relationship management remain.
This is proxy contamination; routine administrative interactions are exposed if applicable.
The transcription-like proxy role is highly exposed to AI draft production and QA tooling.

breakdown_by_role.role: Mail Clerks and Mail Machine Operators, Except Postal Service
breakdown_by_role.rationale: This is proxy contamination; routine mail/document handling is exposed if present.
Tier-one support automates; remote-deposition and workflow integration issues persist.
Residual is assumed to contain a mix of court-production, freelance coordination and routine back-office work.

pi_dist.rationale: An agency can retain workflow and coordination savings, but page-rate and procurement economics limit full retention of lower transcription cost.
caveats: Federal court rates are a pricing-mechanism reference, not a complete representation of private deposition pricing.

pi_moat.rationale: Certified capture, roster depth, chain-of-custody capability and preferred-vendor relationships provide moderate protection, but are target-specific and can be lost if freelancers or clients do not transfer.
caveats: No target-specific reporter agreements, rule coverage or client retention data was reviewed.

t50_years.rationale: Digital reporting is an established pathway, but reliable legal-record production and jurisdictional acceptance slow effective replacement of half of eligible workflow.
caveats: AAERT documents a digital path, not a measured 561492 AI-adoption rate.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Digital reporting and transcription certifications establish a nonzero adoption base, while the public source does not quantify agency-level AI workflow penetration.
caveats: This is effective-work adoption, not the percentage of firms using any AI tool.

historical_analogs.value: Digital audio capture separated proceeding capture from transcription; AI extends that shift by reducing first-draft and cleanup labor while retaining a quality-control and legal-record role.
historical_analogs.rationale: The analogy follows the documented digital-reporting workflow rather than claiming a measured historical elasticity.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Outcome differs by jurisdiction and proceeding type
caveats: Digital reporting is not equivalent to fully autonomous certification.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: AAERT describes retirement-driven stenographer shortages; that supports an aging-workforce concern but does not directly measure agency-owner age, so the owner figure is a cautious proxy.
caveats: Reporter age is not the same as business-owner age.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: AAERT explicitly describes a growing stenographer shortage driven by retirement and failed recruitment; this documents a production-talent shortage relevant to succession/buyability.
caveats: This is evidence of a reporter pipeline shortage, not a census of agency ownership succession plans.

active_consolidators.rationale: Reopened industry sources identify Veritext, Magna, Lexitas, US Legal Support, Esquire and other platforms/regionals as plausible acquirer categories; count is approximate and intended for the buyability rubric.
caveats: This is not a paid transaction-database count and active status varies by geography and target quality.

buy_mult.rationale: A reopened court-reporting valuation guide states 4.5x-6.5x adjusted EBITDA for regional $500K-$2M firms; 5.0x is a conservative point within that published range for a transferable regional target amid AI and roster risk.
caveats: S4 is a commercial valuation guide rather than a disclosed-comps database; the dataset's $1-10M EBITDA band spans regional and platform categories.

exit_mult.rationale: A quality target that builds recurring law-firm relationships, retains reporters and integrates digital workflow can exit nearer the middle of the published regional range; this assumes no terminal automation shock.
caveats: Exit depends on client/roster transferability and is not a guaranteed multiple expansion; source ranges are commercial estimates.

pricing_structure.value: Transcript work is commonly monetized per page with different turnaround and copy rates; agency fees may also include scheduling and related litigation-support services.
pricing_structure.rationale: Primary federal-court examples establish per-page and delivery-rate mechanics, which create a channel for AI production savings to be competed away or retained depending on contract and service mix.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Per-page, appearance, realtime, bundled and negotiated law-firm arrangements
caveats: Federal court schedules do not establish a universal private deposition price or agency margin.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Moderate task exposure and a four-year adoption path support a gradual margin opportunity, not a claim that present savings fully arrive immediately.

terminal_value.justification: AI can reduce transcription and back-office labor, but the code's legal-record, realtime and certified-capture functions retain a paid human residual in important workflows.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: The 4-digit occupational mix, W-2 labor share and commercial valuation guide are disclosed proxies; none is presented as direct proof of a particular agency's reporter mix, cost base or multiple.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Per-page and turnaround pricing creates a capture risk: lower production cost may be retained by the agency or competed into lower client pricing, which is assessed in capture rather than terminal demand.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied 4-digit 5614 role mix is dominated by contact-center occupations and omits court reporters; W-2 labor share may exclude material 1099 reporter payouts and the dataset uses stated cross-vintage inputs.
sensitivity: A true court-reporter/stenographer wage and contractor-cost mix could materially alter V.
The relative mix of deposition reporting and realtime captioning can alter adoption, capture and terminal demand.

market_boundary.selection_basis: Census NAICS definition; generic document transcription and non-live stenography are excluded under Census cross-references.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 3.33 to 1.43 across the stated interval.
V is sensitive to the unrepresentative role mix and unobserved contractor production cost.
M ranges from no spread to a materially positive spread, depending on roster/client transferability.

reviewer_note: Research packet authored from reopened live sources on 2026-07-21; every score-driving source ID was reopened before finalization.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=4, A=2.5
